name: SLB N.V.

profile.description: SLB N.V. (formerly Schlumberger) is the world's largest oilfield services company, operating across four divisions: Digital & Integration, Reservoir Performance, Well Construction, and Production Systems. Founded in 1926 and headquartered in Houston, Texas, the company serves the energy industry in over 100 countries with 109,000 employees. In October 2025, the company formally rebranded from Schlumberger Limited to SLB N.V., reflecting its evolution from an oilfield services company to a global technology company focused on energy innovation. FY2025 revenue was $35.7B with a $82B market cap.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): SLB executed a transformative strategic shift in October 2022, rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB and repositioning as 'a global technology company focused on driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' — a meaningful business model evolution beyond traditional oilfield services [3]. The $7.8B ChampionX acquisition (completed 2025) added production chemicals and digital monitoring, and the Lumi data and AI platform represents a new product category for the energy sector. AI Deployment (1/2): CEO Olivier Le Peuch has emphasized AI leadership, with the Lumi AI platform providing enhanced workflows and decision-making tools for energy operators, and $1.5B+ targeted in digital technology investments [3]. However, specific AI revenue contributions or quantified cost savings have not been separately reported. Market Share (2/2): SLB is the world's largest oilfield services company by revenue ($35.7B vs. HAL's $22.2B), with leading positions in wireline logging, drilling fluids, subsea production systems, and reservoir characterization globally [1]. Product Pipeline (0/2): While the Lumi platform and ChampionX integration are in progress, no specific next-generation products with launch dates and revenue targets were identified in public materials. R&D Intensity (1/1): Technology investment of $1.5B+ annually in digital, AI, and energy transition solutions exceeds oilfield services industry averages [3].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): SLB's purpose — 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' — is more aspirational than typical oilfield services missions, connecting the company's work to the energy transition and sustainability. The October 2022 rebrand validated this as more than a tagline by fundamentally restructuring the company's identity [3]. However, the tension between operating as the world's largest fossil fuel services provider while positioning as an energy transition leader creates credibility questions. Community Impact (1/2): SLB's 'Living our Culture' framework emphasizes diversity and inclusion, and the Board oversees workforce health and safety, human rights, biodiversity, and water resources [4]. MSCI AA ESG rating and Low Controversy level from Sustainalytics validate community practices, but no distinctive charitable programs with specific dollar amounts were identified. Employee Retention (1/2): 109,000 employees with a culture framework that emphasizes 'how diversity, and particularly inclusion, leads to further innovation and performance' [3]. MIT Sloan Executive Education partnership for innovation dialogue suggests investment in talent development. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Le Peuch sold $1.4M in stock in May 2026, and directors also sold modest amounts. Insider positions are maintained through stock awards but no meaningful net buying was identified [2].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored as a dividend stock. D/E of 42.5% is well below the 70% target (score 1.0). ROE of 14.1% is just below the 15% target (score 0.94). Q1 2026 revenue of $8.72B grew 3% YoY with North America up 26% (ChampionX contribution), offset by international decline of 4% due to Middle East disruptions [1]. Revenue 3-year CAGR of 8.3% (score 0.89). Forward P/E of 16.5x is below the 20x target (score 1.0). Profit margin of 9.3% is below the 20% target (score 0.46), reflecting the capital-intensive nature of oilfield services. Payout ratio of 50.7% is above the 40% target (score 0.82, 1x weight). Consecutive years paid: 20+ years including through the 2020 COVID cut-and-recovery (score 1.0, 2x weight). Consecutive years dividend growth: 4 years since 2022 recovery (score 0.40, 1x weight). 5-year dividend growth CAGR of ~17.9% from post-cut recovery far exceeds the 6% target (score 1.0, 2x weight) [2]. Weighted sum: 10.27/12 = Infrastructure 9. Liquidity: $55.12 x 16.5M = $907M, passes floor.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): SLB's core business — providing physical equipment, chemicals, and on-site services for drilling, completing, and producing oil and gas wells — is structurally immune to AI substitution [1]. No AI model can physically drill a well, pump cement casing, manage subsea production trees, or operate wireline logging tools in downhole conditions. The business requires specialized equipment, proprietary chemicals, and thousands of trained field engineers operating in remote and hazardous environments. Regulatory requirements (well safety, environmental compliance) add additional moats. The energy transition actually increases demand for SLB's expertise — carbon capture, geothermal energy, and hydrogen production all require subsurface engineering that SLB uniquely provides. Analysts do not flag AI displacement risk for oilfield services companies. AI Opportunity (1/1): SLB generates quantifiable revenue from its Digital & Integration division, which includes the Lumi AI platform, cloud-based analytics, and AI-enhanced drilling optimization tools [3]. CEO Le Peuch has committed to 'enhancing leadership in AI, cloud computing' and the company invested $1.5B+ in digital technologies in 2024. The Digital & Integration division is a reportable revenue segment, confirming meaningful AI-adjacent revenue.

infinite_game.summary: SLB under Olivier Le Peuch is attempting a genuinely transformative pivot — rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB and repositioning as a technology company for the energy transition rather than a pure oilfield services provider. The 100-year history of continuous operation, consistent technology investment, and willingness to rebrand and restructure the entire company identity demonstrate infinite-game thinking. However, the inherent tension between being the world's largest fossil fuel services provider and claiming an energy transition purpose creates credibility questions that Sinek would probe deeply.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): SLB's rebranded purpose — 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' — is aspirational and connects the company's work to a larger societal challenge (energy transition) rather than just maximizing shareholder returns [3]. The October 2022 rebrand was a significant corporate commitment to this vision, backed by $1.5B+ in digital/transition technology investment. The company has 100 years of continuous operation with consistent commitment to technical innovation. However, SLB remains the world's largest provider of services to fossil fuel extraction — the tension between this reality and the 'balanced planet' purpose has not yet been fully resolved through measurable energy transition revenue. A genuine cause, partially lived.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): SLB's 109,000 employees operate across 100+ countries under the 'Living our Culture' framework that explicitly emphasizes diversity, inclusion, and innovation [3]. The MIT Sloan Executive Education partnership for 'Building a Dialogue for Innovation' suggests genuine investment in employee empowerment and intellectual safety. CEO Le Peuch has held senior roles at the company since joining in 1987, and leadership stability is evident. However, at 109,000 employees in a cyclical industry, workforce reductions during downturns are inevitable and periodic layoffs undermine full trust culture.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): SLB benchmarks itself against Halliburton, Baker Hughes, and increasingly against technology companies as it positions itself in the digital energy space. The company's competitive language — 'driving energy innovation' rather than 'beating competitors' — and the decision to acquire ChampionX's capabilities rather than build them internally reflect a learning-from-others mindset [1][3]. The rebrand itself implicitly acknowledges that the 'Schlumberger' identity needed to evolve in response to industry shifts.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB in October 2022 — redefining a 100-year-old company from 'the world's largest oilfield services company' to 'a global technology company focused on energy innovation for a balanced planet' — represents meaningful existential flexibility [3]. The $7.8B ChampionX acquisition added production optimization and digital monitoring capabilities beyond traditional oilfield services. However, the core business remains fundamentally the same (serving upstream oil and gas operators), and energy transition revenue remains a small fraction of the $35.7B total. The rebrand is aspirational flexibility, not yet transformative flexibility.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Rebranding a 100-year-old company — one of the most recognized names in the energy industry — required genuine courage, as it signaled a strategic direction that could alienate traditional oil and gas clients while not yet securing energy transition credibility [3]. The $1.5B+ digital technology investment and $7.8B ChampionX acquisition during an uncertain commodity price environment demonstrate willingness to invest in long-term capability. However, SLB has not yet walked away from profitable but misaligned businesses — it continues to grow all traditional oilfield services divisions alongside the transition narrative.

investment_thesis: SLB delivered Q1 2026 revenue of $8.72B (+3% YoY), with Production Systems surging 23% to $3.51B driven by ChampionX contribution, and North America revenue up 26% to $2.17B [1]. At 16.5x forward earnings and a 2.14% dividend yield, the stock offers reasonable value for the world's largest oilfield services company with dominant global market share, a clean balance sheet (42.5% D/E), and a 100-year operating history. The Lumi AI platform and $1.5B+ digital technology investment position SLB to capture the energy industry's digital transformation. The ALIGNED Inspire score (+51) and absence of negative attributions make it suitable for faith-based investors, and the dividend has grown at a 17.9% 5-year CAGR from post-COVID recovery levels.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on growth deceleration and margin pressure: FY2025 revenue declined 1.6% from $36.3B to $35.7B, Q1 2026 net income fell 6% to $752M, and EPS declined 14% to $0.50 despite top-line growth [1]. International revenue — representing 74% of total — declined 4% in Q1 due to Middle East disruptions, highlighting geographic concentration risk. Profit margin of only 9.3% reflects the capital-intensive nature of oilfield services, and the payout ratio of 50.7% leaves limited room for dividend growth without earnings improvement. The PEG ratio of 1.89 suggests the stock is not cheap relative to its modest growth profile. The ChampionX acquisition adds integration complexity, and the 'energy transition' rebrand has not yet translated into meaningful transition revenue — the company remains fundamentally dependent on upstream fossil fuel spending cycles. CEO Le Peuch's $1.4M stock sale in May 2026 suggests less than full conviction at current prices [2].
key_catalysts: ChampionX integration synergies materializing — the $7.8B acquisition drove Production Systems revenue up 23% in Q1 2026; full integration could unlock margin improvement and cross-selling across SLB's global client base

Middle East oilfield activity recovery — Q1 2026 Well Construction declined 6% due to regional disruptions; normalization of Saudi Arabia and UAE spending would drive international revenue rebound and EPS recovery

Lumi AI platform and Digital & Integration division revenue acceleration — if SLB can demonstrate AI-driven value creation with specific revenue metrics, it would validate the technology company repositioning and potentially re-rate the multiple
key_risks: FY2025 revenue declined 1.6% and Q1 2026 EPS fell 14% — any further E&P spending cuts from oil price weakness or global recession would compress revenue and margins in the core oilfield services business
International revenue concentration (74% of total) with 4% Q1 decline creates vulnerability to geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East, Russia, and other key operating regions

Energy transition positioning gap: the 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' rebrand has not yet produced material transition revenue, and the company remains fundamentally dependent on fossil fuel services — credibility risk increases if the gap between aspiration and reality persists
